chain_of_thought: Available data for this crash is extremely minimal: the stack trace shows unknown native frames ("??") followed by the JNI bridge method Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor, then fuzz_one_input and main. The only mapped native library is librealm-jni.so exporting that JNI method. No allocator, sanitizer, or specific crashing function is visible, and there is no decompiled code, arguments, or exception text to analyze.

Per the mandatory workflow, I would normally identify the first application-level native frame below allocators/sanitizers in the stack, then use Ghidra MCP to decompile it and trace memcpy/memmove calls and their arguments; from there I would walk backward up the call chain, ultimately reaching the JNI entry and then tracking taint from Java inputs. However, with the given CrashEntry, none of the intermediate native frames are named and no addresses or additional native symbols are provided. That makes concrete MCP actions (search_functions_by_name, decompile_function_by_address, xrefs, etc.) impossible in this context. Likewise, I do not have the Java callgraph beyond the name of the JNI bridge, so I cannot see any Java-level validation or parameter construction.

Despite this limitation, I can reason at a higher level about whether the crash is more likely due to a harness/environment issue or a genuine vulnerability. The fuzz harness entry is fuzz_one_input, which suggests this is a native- or JNI-level fuzzer that may be invoking Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor with arbitrary or highly unrealistic parameters. NativeRawDescriptor in Realm typically handles query descriptors constructed by higher-level Java APIs (ordering, distinct, limit, etc.). In normal app operation, these descriptors are constructed through Realm's Java API, which constrains the types, counts, and structure; direct arbitrary calls into the raw descriptor JNI with malformed or nonsensical arguments are generally not possible from application code without using private/internal APIs or reflection.

Because the fuzzer is driving the JNI bridge directly, it is highly plausible that it is passing null pointers, impossible indices, invalid enum values, or otherwise invalid state that production Java code never provides. Many Realm JNI methods perform runtime checks and may assert or abort if invariants are violated. A crash observed under such fuzzing may reflect assertion aborts or defensive failures when assumptions about internal invariants are broken, rather than a classic memory corruption vulnerability that could be reached under normal app use.

Without a concrete indication of out-of-bounds, use-after-free, integer overflow, or other corruption at an application-level native function, and given that (1) the top native frames are unknown, (2) we have no evidence of allocator/sanitizer messages, and (3) the path is clearly through a fuzz harness directly into an internal JNI entry, the most reasonable classification is that this crash is not yet supported as a genuine vulnerability. It might represent a potential robustness bug or local DoS if an app misuses the internal API, but there is insufficient evidence to claim exploitable memory safety issues. Therefore, I classify it as not-vulnerable with low confidence toward any actual vulnerability, primarily attributing it to the fuzz harness or unrealistic input path.

I will still record that librealm-jni.so and the JNI function Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor are involved, but mark the absence of stack details, code, and taint analysis as major limitations.
reasons: Stack trace lacks concrete crashing function, allocator frame, or error message; only unknown native frames and the JNI bridge are shown
Crash is triggered via fuzz_one_input directly calling an internal JNI method (nativeRawDescriptor) with likely unrealistic parameters not representative of normal app usage
No evidence of memory corruption primitives (OOB, UAF, integer overflow) or attacker-controlled unsafe operations; could be an assertion or invariant violation inside Realm
Insufficient context (no decompiled code, addresses, or java_callgraph) to demonstrate a realistic attacker-controlled path to unsafe behavior
recommendations: Re-run fuzzing with symbolized builds and sanitizers enabled to capture precise crashing function, error type, and allocator diagnostics
Inspect the implementation of Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor in librealm-jni.so for unchecked pointer or length usage, especially with descriptor arrays and indices
Augment the fuzz harness to more closely mimic valid Java-level construction of TableQuery descriptors, filtering out impossible states, then see if crashes persist
assumptions: Fuzz harness directly controls arguments to Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor without normal Realm Java-side validation
The unknown native frames ("??") are internal Realm functions inside librealm-jni.so
No additional allocator/sanitizer logs or Java callgraph information are available beyond what is shown
limitations: No function names, addresses, or decompiled native code for the crashing frame are provided, preventing concrete data-flow analysis
No Java callgraph or source for the JNI bridge and its callers, so attacker control and validation of arguments cannot be precisely assessed
No crash reason (signal type, abort message, sanitizer report) is available to distinguish memory corruption from assertion or logic aborts
